title: World Diving Calendar
description: Year-round diving conditions for global destinations, organized by month. Each destination includes monthly surface water temperature (degC), currents, marine-life highlights, conditions/visibility and a season rating (Peak / Good / Shoulder / Low / Closed).

rating_legend.Peak: Best conditions and/or signature marine life
rating_legend.Closed: Not accessible (park closure or no operators)

destinations.name: Sipadan Island

destinations.name: Great Barrier Reef

destinations.highlights: World's largest reef; dwarf minke whales Jun-Jul; coral spawning Nov.

Nov.conditions: Late spring; warming; stinger season starts

Dec.marine_life: Green turtle nesting (Raine Is); coral spawning spill
Dec.conditions: Early summer wet; warm; stingers; cyclone risk

destinations.name: Komodo National Park
destinations.currents: Tidal, strongest at full/new moon; powerful drift; Castle Rock/Crystal Rock (reef-hook pinnacles), Batu Bolong (ferocious flank currents), The Cauldron/Shotgun (committed drift); downcurrents at pinnacles. N warm/calm, S cold upwelling/rough
destinations.highlights: Strong tidal drifts; warm north vs cold-upwelling south; mantas year-round by zone.

Jan.marine_life: South mantas PEAK (Manta Alley); hammerheads (south); macro explosion
Jan.conditions: Wet; lower viz (5-15m), choppier north; Dec can be 30m+

Feb.marine_life: South manta PEAK (Jan-Feb); macro (Rhinopias, frogfish); hammerheads
Feb.conditions: Wet; lower viz; southern manta aggregations largest

Mar.marine_life: South mantas; hammerheads (to early Apr); rich macro

Apr.marine_life: N/Central mantas active; whale sharks (Apr-Jun); reef sharks

May.marine_life: N/Central mantas; whale sharks; reef sharks

Jul.marine_life: N mantas; reef sharks; mola season nears

Nov.marine_life: South mantas building (Manta Alley); reef sharks

Dec.marine_life: South manta peak begins; macro; reef sharks; dragons active
Dec.conditions: Wet; lower viz but can be 30m+; choppier north

dive_sites.blurb: Gentle east-coast bommies and white sand; classic check-out dive

dive_sites.name: West of Eden
dive_sites.blurb: Boulders and coral gardens off Island 7, rich macro and reef fish

dive_sites.name: Christmas Point
dive_sites.blurb: Boulder swim-throughs at Island 9 with sea fans and leopard sharks

dive_sites.name: Boulder City
dive_sites.blurb: Submerged boulder field south of Island 3, pelagics in current

dive_sites.name: Shark Fin Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Long granite ridge with drift along sloping coral; napoleon wrasse

dive_sites.name: Deep Six
dive_sites.blurb: Deep boulder formations and arches at Island 7's northern tip

destinations.name: Tubbataha Reefs Natural Park
destinations.currents: Strong oceanic currents; drift diving common on the walls
destinations.highlights: UNESCO atoll with sharks, mantas and pristine walls, liveaboard-only short season
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Ron Van Oers (CC BY-SA 3.0 igo) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.current_note: Persistent currents; all dives drift with live boat

dive_sites.name: Amos Rock
dive_sites.blurb: Gorgonian-draped wall with huge fish schools and patrolling reef sharks

dive_sites.name: Shark Airport
dive_sites.blurb: Broad plateau cleaning station where whitetip sharks rest in numbers

dive_sites.name: Washing Machine
dive_sites.blurb: Unpredictable currents drawing grey reefs, mantas and hammerheads

dive_sites.name: Delsan Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: South Atoll wreckage swarmed by snappers, eagle rays and three shark species

dive_sites.name: Malayan Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: Grounded wreck and reef with fine macro, nudibranchs and crustaceans

dive_sites.name: Jessie Beazley Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Isolated reef crown north of the atolls with superb visibility

dive_sites.name: Staghorn Point
dive_sites.blurb: Fields of staghorn coral with turtles and schooling reef fish

dive_sites.name: Black Rock
dive_sites.blurb: South Atoll anchorage wall with sharks, rays and pelagic action

destinations.name: Lord Howe Island
destinations.currents: Protected lagoon plus exposed sites with moderate current/surge
destinations.highlights: World's southernmost coral reef blending tropical and temperate species
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Toby Hudson (CC BY-SA 3.0 au) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.current_note: Island sites sheltered; strong drift at Ball's Pyramid

dive_sites.name: Ball's Pyramid
dive_sites.blurb: World's tallest sea stack; Galapagos whalers, kingfish, rare angelfish

dive_sites.name: Admiralty Islands
dive_sites.blurb: Volcanic islets with 30+ sites of pinnacles, canyons and caves

dive_sites.name: Erscott's Hole
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered 8m lagoon hole; doubleheader wrasse and spangled emperor

dive_sites.name: Comets Hole
dive_sites.blurb: Coral-ringed lagoon hole teeming with resident reef fish

dive_sites.name: Tenth of June
dive_sites.blurb: Admiralty islet dive with swim-throughs and dense fish life

dive_sites.name: Rupert's Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Classic outer-reef coral dive on the world's southernmost coral reef

dive_sites.name: Deacon's Delight
dive_sites.blurb: Popular boat-dive reef among Lord Howe's 60+ charted sites

destinations.name: South West Rocks
destinations.currents: Fish Rock Cave can have strong tidal currents and surge
destinations.best_months: May-Dec (grey nurse aggregations); Feb-Apr warmest water
destinations.highlights: Fish Rock Cave swim-through with grey nurse shark aggregations

Jan.conditions: Warmest, EAC viz, can be strong current

Feb.marine_life: Warm EAC; turtles, rays; fewer grey nurse

Mar.marine_life: Turtles, rays; grey nurse returning late month

Oct.marine_life: Grey nurse resting in cave gutters; rays

Dec.marine_life: Grey nurse in shallow gutters; turtles

destinations.current_note: EAC variable; Fish Rock has sheltered gutters

dive_sites.name: Fish Rock Cave
dive_sites.blurb: 125m ocean cave through Fish Rock; grey nurse sharks at both entrances

dive_sites.name: Shark Gutters
dive_sites.blurb: 15-30m canyons at Fish Rock patrolled by critically endangered grey nurses

dive_sites.name: The Aquarium
dive_sites.blurb: Fish-packed SW corner of Fish Rock near the cave's shallow entrance

dive_sites.name: The Pinnacle
dive_sites.blurb: Sheer Fish Rock walls overlooking gutters holding 20-30 grey nurse sharks

dive_sites.name: Green Island
dive_sites.blurb: Grey nurse critical habitat islet near Smoky Cape, 14-18m rocky reef

dive_sites.name: Black Rock
dive_sites.blurb: Rocky reef 8-20m; rays, schooling morwong, moray eels and lionfish

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Photographer: Richard Ling <wikipedia@rling.com> (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Nusa Penida
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm (bring 5mm for Jul-Oct upwellings, which can drop to 15-18C at Crystal Bay depths)
destinations.current_note: Fast drifts and occasional down-currents at Crystal Bay, Toyapakeh and the north-coast points
destinations.currents: The Indonesian Throughflow funnels between the islands, driving fast, shifting drifts and sudden down-currents that demand solid buoyancy and guide-led planning.
destinations.highlights: Oceanic sunfish on cold winter upwellings and reef mantas cruising the cleaning stations all year, wrapped in some of Bali's wildest drift diving.

Jan.marine_life: Reef mantas active at Manta Point on rich wet-season nutrients; turtles and reef fish
Jan.conditions: Wet season; afternoon rain and strong currents, morning dives usually fine

Feb.marine_life: Mantas year-round at the cleaning stations; hawksbill turtles and macro on protected reefs
Feb.conditions: Wet season; plankton-rich water, currents can pick up

Mar.marine_life: Reliable manta rays plus eagle rays and reef sharks along the walls
Mar.conditions: End of wet season; seas settling, occasional afternoon showers

Apr.marine_life: Mantas at Manta Point, whitetip reef sharks and turtles on the drifts
Apr.conditions: Dry season begins; calmer seas and clearing water

May.marine_life: Year-round mantas plus bumphead parrotfish and reef sharks on the north points
May.conditions: Dry season; predictable conditions and strong drift diving

Jun.marine_life: Mantas cruising; first cool upwellings raise early mola-mola hopes at Crystal Bay
Jun.conditions: Dry season; strengthening currents as the Throughflow peaks

Jul.marine_life: Mola-mola season opens at Crystal Bay; mantas steady at Manta Point
Jul.conditions: Dry season; cold upwellings, punchy currents, excellent visibility

Aug.marine_life: Prime mola-mola sightings on cold water; mantas year-round on the reefs
Aug.conditions: Dry season; strongest upwellings, cool water and clear seas

Sep.marine_life: Best-odds mola-mola month alongside reliable reef mantas
Sep.conditions: Dry season; peak upwelling, cool water and top visibility

Oct.marine_life: Mola-mola sightings tapering as upwelling weakens; mantas still steady
Oct.conditions: Dry season closing; water warming, currents easing, clear seas

Nov.marine_life: Mantas active on returning nutrients; turtles and reef fish
Nov.conditions: Wet season starting; rough seas and strong currents possible

Dec.marine_life: Year-round mantas at Manta Point plus reef sharks and macro life
Dec.conditions: Wet season; afternoon rain, plankton-rich water, brisk currents

dive_sites.name: Crystal Bay
dive_sites.blurb: The classic mola-mola site, where cold Jul-Oct upwellings and possible down-currents bring sunfish to cleaning stations on the deep slope.

dive_sites.name: Manta Point
dive_sites.blurb: Reef mantas glide over cleaning stations year-round on the exposed south coast, often in surge and swell.

dive_sites.name: Manta Bay
dive_sites.blurb: A shallow bay where schooling reef mantas feed and get cleaned, a calmer alternative to Manta Point.

dive_sites.name: Toyapakeh
dive_sites.blurb: A vivid coral wall in the channel with fast currents and occasional down-currents, and a chance of mola-mola in the deep.

dive_sites.name: SD Point
dive_sites.blurb: A north-coast high-speed drift over sloping reef with turtles, reef sharks and big schools when the current runs.

dive_sites.name: Gamat Bay
dive_sites.blurb: A sheltered white-sand bay with coral bommies, strong macro life and clear water, best for calmer conditions.

dive_sites.name: Ceningan Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A dramatic drop-off between Penida and Ceningan with strong currents, big fans and pelagic action for experienced divers.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Andrepiazza (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Fuvahmulah
destinations.best_months: Dec-Apr (calm dry-season seas and best visibility; tiger sharks year-round)
destinations.current_note: open blue-water drop-offs, tide-driven
destinations.currents: This is exposed oceanic diving off a single deep-water island, where tidal currents sweep the drop-offs and blue-water dives are done as drifts.
destinations.highlights: The tiger shark capital of the world, where a shallow harbour plateau puts you eye-to-eye with resident tigers while deep drop-offs deliver threshers, oceanic mantas and schooling hammerheads.

Jan.marine_life: Resident tiger sharks daily; scalloped hammerheads still schooling and silvertips gathering; whale sharks possible along the drop-offs.
Jan.conditions: Peak northeast-monsoon dry season with calm seas and excellent clarity.

Feb.marine_life: Tiger sharks year-round; strong window for silvertips (sometimes in the hundreds), hammerhead schools and whale sharks.
Feb.conditions: Dry northeast monsoon, settled surface and gin-clear water.

Mar.marine_life: Widest diversity of the year: tigers daily, hammerheads still schooling, oceanic manta season opening, threshers on the cleaning stations and whale sharks around.
Mar.conditions: Late dry season, warm calm seas and top visibility.

Apr.marine_life: Tigers year-round; oceanic mantas building toward their mating peak, with whale sharks and lingering hammerheads.
Apr.conditions: Tail of the dry northeast monsoon, still calm before the seasonal change.

May.marine_life: Tiger sharks daily; peak oceanic manta season as they gather to mate, clean and feed.
May.conditions: Southwest monsoon setting in with the warmest water and increasingly variable surface.

Jun.marine_life: Reliable resident tiger sharks; threshers on the deep cleaning stations and passing oceanic mantas.
Jun.conditions: Wet southwest monsoon, rougher surface and reduced visibility but quieter sites.

Jul.marine_life: Tiger sharks year-round; threshers at depth and occasional pelagic passers-by.
Jul.conditions: Southwest monsoon, wetter and choppier with more current.

Aug.marine_life: Guaranteed resident tigers; steady thresher shark encounters on the reef cleaning stations.
Aug.conditions: Peak wet southwest monsoon, unsettled surface and greener water.

Sep.marine_life: Tiger sharks daily; threshers reliable and some years extra pelagic action on the quieter sites.
Sep.conditions: Southwest monsoon easing, still wet with variable visibility.

Oct.marine_life: Tigers year-round; thresher sharks entering their strongest window and scalloped hammerhead schools beginning to build.
Oct.conditions: Monsoon transition, seas starting to settle and clarity improving.

Nov.marine_life: Resident tigers daily; prime thresher season continues and hammerhead schools grow as the thermocline shallows.
Nov.conditions: Early dry northeast monsoon, calmer surface and rising visibility.

Dec.marine_life: Tigers year-round; thresher sharks at their peak and dependable schooling hammerheads.
Dec.conditions: Northeast monsoon dry season returning with calm seas and clear water.

dive_sites.name: Tiger Harbour
dive_sites.blurb: The harbour-mouth plateau that made Fuvahmulah famous, where named resident tiger sharks patrol at close range in shallow, clear water.

dive_sites.name: Tiger Zoo
dive_sites.blurb: The most consistent spot to sit with numbers of resident tigers throughout the year, often with silvertips and nurse sharks in the mix.

dive_sites.name: Farikede
dive_sites.blurb: Southern reef with thresher shark cleaning stations that rise from deep water, one of the rarest big-animal encounters in the Maldives.

dive_sites.name: Rasgefanno
dive_sites.blurb: Rocky-bottomed stage for tiger sharks alongside silvertips and leopard sharks, with oceanic mantas occasionally cruising past.

dive_sites.name: Neregando Fannu
dive_sites.blurb: Steep oceanic wall drifted in current for hammerhead schools, oceanic mantas and passing pelagics off the deep blue.

dive_sites.name: Thoondu
dive_sites.blurb: Northern point off the famous white-pebble beach, an exposed corner that channels current and pelagic traffic.

dive_sites.name: Razwa Faru
dive_sites.blurb: Southern deep reef and drop-off known for threshers on the cleaning stations plus silvertips and schooling hammerheads.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Marion Kraschl (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Layang Layang
destinations.current_note: Wall sites can run strong; sheltered lagoon dives are calm
destinations.currents: Currents range from mild inside the atoll to strong along the exposed drop-offs where the pelagics gather.
destinations.highlights: A remote oceanic atoll where walls plunge 2,000 m and springtime brings schooling scalloped hammerheads in gin-clear water.

Feb.conditions: Monsoon tailing off; resort still closed

Mar.marine_life: Schooling scalloped hammerheads arrive, pelagics on the walls
Mar.conditions: Season opens; flat seas and 30m+ visibility

Apr.marine_life: Hammerhead aggregations at their best, mantas and grey reef sharks

May.marine_life: Hammerhead schooling continues, dogtooth tuna and barracuda

Jun.marine_life: Reef sharks, turtles, big schools of jacks and tuna

Jul.marine_life: Grey reef sharks, mantas, resident pelagics

Aug.marine_life: Reef sharks, turtles, macro on the reef flats
Aug.conditions: Season winding down; occasional wind and swell

dive_sites.name: Gorgonian Forest
dive_sites.blurb: Sea-fan draped drop-off with strong current - the prime spot for schooling hammerheads

dive_sites.name: Dogtooth Lair
dive_sites.blurb: Deep-water wall patrolled by big dogtooth tuna, barracuda and jacks

dive_sites.name: The Valley
dive_sites.blurb: Hard-coral slope with snappers, sweetlips and turtles; hammerheads pass in spring

dive_sites.name: Shark's Cave
dive_sites.blurb: Overhangs sheltering resting whitetip, nurse and leopard sharks

dive_sites.name: D-Wall
dive_sites.blurb: Sheer vertical wall dropping into 2,000 m of blue - a magnet for pelagics

dive_sites.name: Wreck Point
dive_sites.blurb: Reef corner near the resort channel with reef sharks and cruising trevally

dive_sites.name: Snapper Ledges
dive_sites.blurb: Easy sheltered coral garden thick with snapper and colourful reef fish

destinations.image_credit: Photo: matthew lee (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Malpelo Island
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm (7mm or hooded for Dec-May thermoclines down to ~14C)
destinations.current_note: Fierce; reef hooks and drift skills essential
destinations.currents: Powerful, shifting currents and surge scour the exposed pinnacles and walls, so reef hooks, good buoyancy and solid drift-diving skills are mandatory.
destinations.highlights: Walls of 200-plus schooling scalloped hammerheads, silky shark hordes and the rare smalltooth sand tiger on a remote volcanic seamount 500km offshore.

Jan.marine_life: Huge scalloped hammerhead schools; smalltooth sand tiger rises to divable depth; Galapagos sharks
Jan.conditions: Dry season, strong currents, cool shallow thermoclines (~14-18C), plankton cuts viz

Feb.marine_life: Dense hammerhead schools; smalltooth sand tiger at El Monstruo; Galapagos sharks
Feb.conditions: Dry season, powerful currents, cold thermoclines, greenish plankton-rich water

Mar.marine_life: Hammerhead schools still numerous; smalltooth sand tiger; Galapagos sharks
Mar.conditions: Dry season winding down, strong currents, cool water, low-to-moderate viz

Apr.marine_life: Hammerheads present; silky shark numbers building; eagle rays
Apr.conditions: Transition, easing currents, warming water, viz improving

May.marine_life: Hammerheads; silky shark aggregations increasing; Galapagos sharks
May.conditions: Warmer, deeper thermoclines, calmer seas, clearer water

Jun.marine_life: Hammerheads, big silky shark schools and first whale sharks converge
Jun.conditions: Wet season onset, warm water, deep thermoclines, good viz

Jul.marine_life: Whale sharks arriving; hundreds of silky sharks; hammerhead schools
Jul.conditions: Warm, calm seas, occasional rain, strong viz, best all-round window

Aug.marine_life: Whale sharks; massive silky shark aggregations; hammerheads
Aug.conditions: Warmest water (~27C), calm seas, excellent blue-water viz

Sep.marine_life: Whale sharks; silky shark schools; hammerheads
Sep.conditions: Warm, wet-season rains topside, calm, superb viz

Oct.marine_life: Late whale sharks; silky sharks tapering; hammerheads
Oct.conditions: Warm, rainier topside, generally calm, good viz

Nov.marine_life: Hammerhead schools rebuilding; Galapagos and silky sharks
Nov.conditions: Transition to dry season, currents strengthening, viz still good

Dec.marine_life: Largest scalloped hammerhead schools of the year; Galapagos sharks
Dec.conditions: Dry season, strong currents returning, cooling thermoclines, viz dropping

dive_sites.name: La Nevera (The Fridge)
dive_sites.blurb: Signature cleaning station on the northeast side where scalloped hammerheads and Galapagos sharks queue among reef fish; cold upwelling gives it its name.

dive_sites.name: El Bajo del Monstruo (Monster Seamount)
dive_sites.blurb: Offshore pinnacle famous for hundreds of hammerheads and the elusive smalltooth sand tiger that rises to ~21m in the cold season; divable only in good conditions.

dive_sites.name: La Gringa
dive_sites.blurb: Exposed monolith on the south side pierced by a swim-through cave between 24-40m; hammerheads, Galapagos and silky sharks, tuna and eagle rays in fierce current.

dive_sites.name: El Freezer
dive_sites.blurb: Volcanic wall partly clad in hard coral, named for the cold water on this flank; walls of hammerheads and passing pelagics dropping into the blue.

dive_sites.name: Los Tres Mosqueteros
dive_sites.blurb: Trio of rocks off the north coast delivering classic pinnacle diving with silky and Galapagos sharks plus schooling tuna.

dive_sites.name: Sahara
dive_sites.blurb: Southern site where silky sharks congregate in large numbers over a current-swept slope.

dive_sites.name: El Altar de la Virgen
dive_sites.blurb: Colorful hard-coral pinnacle reliable for hammerhead sightings and cruising eagle rays.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Jdmacarenoq (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Yap
destinations.current_note: short (channel currents draw mantas)
destinations.currents: Tidal currents funnel through the reef channels and switch with the tide, so most manta dives are timed as short drifts or hooked hangs at the cleaning stations.
destinations.highlights: Resident reef mantas queue at channel cleaning stations year-round, peaking in the Dec-Apr mating season, with dusk mandarinfish and shark-lined caverns on the side.

Jan.marine_life: Peak manta season: courting trains of resident reef mantas stack up at Mi'l Channel cleaning stations.
Jan.conditions: Dry season, calm seas and minimal current; typhoons rare (tracks pass north of Yap).

Feb.marine_life: Manta mating in full swing with regular multi-ray gatherings on the barrier reef.
Feb.conditions: Dry season peak, calm and settled with excellent clarity.

Mar.marine_life: Mantas still aggregating for the mating season; reef sharks and mandarinfish reliable.
Mar.conditions: Late dry season, calm water and long visibility.

Apr.marine_life: Tail end of the manta mating peak; cleaning stations remain busy.
Apr.conditions: Dry-to-wet transition begins; mostly calm with the first passing showers.

May.marine_life: Resident mantas cruising cleaning stations; macro and reef life steady.
May.conditions: Transition to wet season, occasional showers and slightly choppier surface.

Jun.marine_life: Mantas shift toward Goofnuw and Mi'l channels for summer morning cleanings.
Jun.conditions: Early wet season, more rain and building plankton; typhoon risk stays low.

Jul.marine_life: Plankton-rich water keeps mantas feeding; pelagics visit the outer walls.
Jul.conditions: Wet season core, frequent rain and stronger channel currents.

Aug.marine_life: Summer manta cleanings continue in the channels alongside sharks and eagle rays.
Aug.conditions: Wet season, warmest water, greener plankton-laden vis and firm currents.

Sep.marine_life: Mantas still congregating in Goofnuw through early fall; macro life strong.
Sep.conditions: Late wet season, showery with moderate currents; typhoons uncommon here.

Oct.marine_life: Resident mantas at cleaning stations as summer aggregations wind down.
Oct.conditions: Wet-to-dry transition, seas calming and visibility rebuilding.

Nov.marine_life: Mantas back on the barrier-reef stations as the winter build-up starts.
Nov.conditions: Early dry season, drier and calmer with improving clarity.

Dec.marine_life: Start of the manta mating peak: courting trains form in Mi'l Channel.
Dec.conditions: Dry season, calm and clear with light currents.

dive_sites.name: Mi'l Channel
dive_sites.blurb: A 9m coral ridge squeezes the tidal flow into a natural cleaning station where up to 30 resident mantas hover to be groomed by wrasse, at its busiest during the Dec-Apr mating season.

dive_sites.name: Valley of the Rays
dive_sites.blurb: Coral outcrops rising from the sandy channel floor, including the lettuce-coral 'Merry-Go-Round', act as cleaning stations where mantas queue and hang almost motionless overhead.

dive_sites.name: Goofnuw Channel
dive_sites.blurb: Summer and early-fall manta hotspot where the rays line up each morning at the cleaning stations as the tide pushes through the channel.

dive_sites.name: Yap Caverns
dive_sites.blurb: Canyons, chimneys and chasms in the coral with whitetip reef sharks resting in sandy bowls, grey reef sharks patrolling the outer mini-wall and lionfish tucked in the crevices.

dive_sites.name: Lionfish Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A crevice-riddled wall named for its resident lionfish, with mantas, eagle rays, turtles, sharks and tuna cruising the adjacent blue.

dive_sites.name: Rainbow Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow coral rubble where divers gather at dusk to watch mandarinfish pair off and spiral up into the water column in their nightly mating ritual.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Original uploader was Bartek.cieslak at pl.wikipedia (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Cenderawasih Bay
destinations.best_months: Jun-Oct (whale sharks year-round; best liveaboard season)
destinations.current_note: calm, almost currentless
destinations.currents: Sheltered by its near-enclosed geography, the bay stays calm and virtually currentless, so the shallow whale-shark and wreck dives feel effortless.
destinations.highlights: The only place on Earth to scuba year-round with resident whale sharks feeding under fishermen's bagan platforms, plus coral-clad WWII wrecks in glassy, currentless water.

Jan.marine_life: Whale sharks feed under the Kwatisore bagans year-round; wrecks and endemic reef fish active
Jan.conditions: Wetter season; bay stays calm but open-water transits can be lumpy, so fewer liveaboards run

Feb.marine_life: Resident whale sharks under the bagans; hard-coral gardens on the Shinwa Maru and endemics
Feb.conditions: Wet-season swell offshore; sheltered inner-bay dives remain flat and warm

Mar.marine_life: Whale sharks year-round at the platforms; macro and endemic reef life on the walls
Mar.conditions: Tail of the wetter season; rougher transits limit liveaboard scheduling

Apr.marine_life: Whale sharks under the bagans; wrecks and soft-coral reefs teeming with fish
Apr.conditions: Seas begin to settle as the dry season opens; comfortable liveaboard conditions returning

May.marine_life: Whale sharks reliable at Kwatisore; barracuda and trevally schools on the reefs
May.conditions: Dry-season calm building; warm water and easy shallow dives

Jun.marine_life: Peak whale-shark encounters under the bagans plus wrecks and endemic reef fish
Jun.conditions: Prime liveaboard season begins; calmest seas and driest weather

Jul.marine_life: Multiple whale sharks feeding under the platforms; thriving coral-encrusted wrecks
Jul.conditions: Peak season; glassy water, minimal current, top visibility

Aug.marine_life: Resident whale sharks under the bagans; barrel sponges, endemics and wreck life
Aug.conditions: Peak liveaboard season; calm, dry and highly reliable

Sep.marine_life: Whale sharks at the platforms plus WWII wrecks and soft-coral reefs
Sep.conditions: Excellent late-dry conditions; flat seas and strong visibility

Oct.marine_life: Whale sharks year-round; healthy reefs and coral-clad wrecks
Oct.conditions: End of peak season; still calm and clear before the wetter months

Nov.marine_life: Whale sharks continue under the bagans; wrecks and endemics active
Nov.conditions: Transition into the wetter season; inner bay stays calm, transits get lumpier

Dec.marine_life: Resident whale sharks feed under the platforms all year; reef and wreck life steady
Dec.conditions: Wetter season underway; sheltered dives calm but fewer boats operate

dive_sites.name: Kwatisore Bagans
dive_sites.blurb: The signature dive: divers descend beside the anchored fishing platforms where resident whale sharks hang vertically to suck baitfish from the nets, giving unhurried, close scuba encounters in warm, currentless water.

dive_sites.name: Tanjung Mangguar
dive_sites.blurb: One of the bay's prettiest reefs, blanketed in soft coral and swarmed by barracuda, trevally, fusiliers and surgeonfish along an easy sloping wall.

dive_sites.name: Shinwa Maru
dive_sites.blurb: A 120m Japanese WWII cargo ship lying on its port side, largely intact and now a coral-encrusted reef draped in hard corals and hosting dense fish life.

dive_sites.name: Cross Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A shallow, easy Japanese wartime wreck coated in hard coral and marked by a memorial cross, ideal for relaxed exploration and macro life.

dive_sites.name: Tridacna Atoll
dive_sites.blurb: Deep drop-offs and fringing reefs carpeted in soft corals, sea whips, giant clams and massive barrel and elephant-ear sponges.

dive_sites.name: P-40 Tomahawk Plane Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A remarkably preserved WWII fighter aircraft resting upright with instrumentation and pilot's seat still in place, sitting quietly on the calm bay floor.

dive_sites.name: Purup Island Reefs
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered fringing reefs rich in West Papua's endemic reef fish, healthy hard corals and abundant macro subjects in flat, easy conditions.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Bird's Head Seascape / Jones/Shimlock / Secret Sea Visions (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Jupiter
destinations.best_months: Jan-Mar (hammerheads, lemon sharks); Aug-Sep (goliath grouper spawn)
destinations.current_note: Gulf Stream drift dives; boats live-follow divers, hooks not used
destinations.currents: Persistent north-flowing Gulf Stream drift; often 1-2+ knots
destinations.highlights: Gulf Stream drift diving with winter great hammerheads and lemon shark aggregations, plus a late-summer goliath grouper spawn on the wrecks

Jan.marine_life: Lemon shark aggregation peaks, great hammerheads, bull sharks
Jan.conditions: Cooler Gulf Stream, brisk drift, viz variable with fronts

Feb.marine_life: Lemon sharks, great hammerheads, bull sharks on shark dives
Feb.conditions: Winter shark season, strong drift, cooler water

Mar.marine_life: Great hammerheads, lemon sharks tapering, bull sharks
Mar.conditions: Tail of shark season, drift diving, improving viz

Apr.marine_life: Reef sharks, turtles, rays, resident goliaths
Apr.conditions: Warming water, steady drift, good viz

May.marine_life: Turtles, reef sharks, rays, snook and jacks on wrecks
May.conditions: Warm, calmer seas, clear Gulf Stream drift

Jun.marine_life: Turtles nesting, reef fish, goliaths gathering on wrecks
Jun.conditions: Warm, generally clear, occasional summer storms

Jul.marine_life: Goliath groupers building on wrecks, turtles, reef sharks
Jul.conditions: Warmest, calm, good viz, afternoon storms

Aug.marine_life: Goliath grouper spawning aggregation, 60+ on wrecks
Aug.conditions: Warm, mild drift, clear water; deep wreck profiles

Sep.marine_life: Goliath grouper spawn peaks around new moon, big schools
Sep.conditions: Warm, clear, calm; hurricane season watch

Oct.marine_life: Goliaths thinning, reef sharks, turtles, big bait schools
Oct.conditions: Warm, drift steady, viz good between fronts

Nov.marine_life: Reef sharks, turtles, first winter sharks arriving
Nov.conditions: Cooling, more fronts and swell, viz variable

Dec.marine_life: Lemon sharks arriving, bull sharks, early hammerheads
Dec.conditions: Winter shark season begins, cooler, brisk drift

dive_sites.name: Jupiter Wreck Trek (Zion Train)
dive_sites.blurb: Drift across three wrecks - the Zion freighter, the Miss Jenny barge and the upright Esso Bonaire - a goliath grouper stronghold in the spawn

dive_sites.name: The Ledge
dive_sites.blurb: West-facing double-shelf ledge system with drop-offs 2-4.5 m high, home to reef sharks, turtles and dense fish life on the drift

dive_sites.name: Captain Mike's
dive_sites.blurb: Ledge site on the same reef line as Area 51 and Scarface, good for goliaths, sharks and passing pelagics

dive_sites.name: MG-111
dive_sites.blurb: Broken-up Mississippi River barge north of Jupiter Inlet in about 20 m, a reliable goliath grouper haven

dive_sites.name: Lemon Drop
dive_sites.blurb: Signature winter shark drift where lemon sharks aggregate in numbers, with great hammerheads and bull sharks in season

dive_sites.name: Tunnels / Loggerhead Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Ledge and swim-through reef stretch running with turtles, morays and reef fish on an easy Gulf Stream drift

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Clinton & Charles Robertson from RAF Lakenheath, UK & San Marcos, TX, USA & UK (CC BY-SA 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Protea Banks
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm full year-round; 7mm or hooded 5mm in winter for cold thermoclines, 3mm possible on warmest summer days
destinations.current_note: Offshore fossil-dune reef 7-8 km out swept by the Agulhas Current; drift dives are the norm and current can run hard north or south, sometimes shifting mid-dive.
destinations.currents: Strong, often unpredictable drift; blue-water descents and negative entries are standard, and dives are frequently ended with a free ascent in open water.
destinations.highlights: South Africa's shark-diving capital: a deep 27-40 m reef of caves, pinnacles and gullies where Zambezi (bull) sharks patrol year-round, wintering ragged-tooth sharks pack the caves, and summer brings tiger sharks and schools of scalloped hammerheads. The sardine run passes offshore in June-July. Strictly for experienced, current-comfortable divers.

Jan.marine_life: Bull (Zambezi) sharks patrol the Southern Pinnacles; tail end of the scalloped hammerhead season with tiger sharks possible; game fish plentiful.
Jan.conditions: Warm summer water and generally good visibility, though plankton and runoff can occasionally cloud it; strong drift.

Feb.marine_life: Bull sharks reliable; tiger sharks and the odd late hammerhead; warm-water pelagics and big game-fish shoals.
Feb.conditions: Peak summer warmth; visibility variable but often strong on clear days; expect powerful current.

Mar.marine_life: Bull and tiger sharks continue; autumn transition brings broad species diversity on a single dive.
Mar.conditions: Warm water lingers; sea state and visibility more variable in the transitional season.

Apr.marine_life: First male ragged-tooth sharks begin arriving at the Northern Pinnacles; bull sharks still present.
Apr.conditions: Cooling water and often calmer seas; winter clarity starting to build.

May.marine_life: Ragged-tooth sharks gathering in the caves ahead of the winter mating aggregation; bull sharks year-round.
May.conditions: Drier winter season improving visibility; cooler water, thermoclines appearing.

Jun.marine_life: Ragged-tooth sharks pack the Northern caves in mating numbers; bull sharks at their most consistent; the sardine run passes offshore drawing predators.
Jun.conditions: Clear, dry winter conditions and the best visibility of the year; cold thermoclines, strong drift.

Jul.marine_life: Peak of the ragged-tooth mating aggregation in the caves; bull sharks reliable; sardine run and its predator trains active offshore.
Jul.conditions: Cold, clear winter water with excellent visibility; brisk current on the deep reef.

Aug.marine_life: Ragged-tooth sharks still in good numbers in the Northern caves; bull sharks throughout; guitarfish on the sand.
Aug.conditions: Clear winter water; cold on the bottom, strong drift dives.

Sep.marine_life: Late ragged-tooth sharks overlap with the first scalloped hammerheads returning; bull sharks constant.
Sep.conditions: Warming water, good clarity; current can run hard either direction.

Oct.marine_life: Bull sharks reliable; scalloped hammerhead schools building; tiger sharks reappearing with the warmth.
Oct.conditions: Spring transition, warming water, generally good visibility.

Nov.marine_life: Huge schools of scalloped hammerheads at their most regular; bull and tiger sharks over the Southern Pinnacles.
Nov.conditions: Warm summer water returning; strong drift, visibility usually good.

Dec.marine_life: Peak scalloped hammerhead schooling with bull and tiger sharks; the widest summer pelagic mix over the Southern Pinnacles.
Dec.conditions: Warm water and often excellent visibility, though plankton blooms can intervene; powerful drift.

dive_sites.name: Northern Pinnacles
dive_sites.blurb: The colder-water end of the reef and the heart of the winter ragged-tooth season, a series of pinnacles, ledges and cave systems where 'raggies' shelter from the current in June-November.

dive_sites.name: Southern Pinnacles
dive_sites.blurb: The summer pelagic arena, dived as a drift over pinnacles and sand in search of bull and tiger sharks and schooling scalloped hammerheads.

dive_sites.name: Sharks' Gullies (Northern Caves)
dive_sites.blurb: Twin overhang and cave systems in the Northern Pinnacles where dozens of ragged-tooth sharks congregate at close quarters during the winter mating aggregation.

dive_sites.name: Southern Cave
dive_sites.blurb: The usual start of the Southern Pinnacle dive, a rock overhang packed with game fish that opens onto the drift toward the gullies and sand.

dive_sites.name: Kingfish Gully
dive_sites.blurb: An overhanging rock beyond the Southern Cave holding dense shoals of kingfish, yellowtail and potato bass, with sharks often cruising the edge.

dive_sites.name: Sand Shark Gully (The Arena)
dive_sites.blurb: A large sandy patch at the reef's deepest, roughly 40 m, known as the Arena and home to giant guitarfish; a classic spot to hang for passing pelagics.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Willemferguson2 (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Tofo
destinations.best_months: Oct-Mar (whale sharks + mantas, warm water, 20-30m viz)
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm (add hood and vest in Jun-Aug winter)
destinations.current_note: Outer reefs (Manta Reef, Giants Castle, The Office) are drift dives with moderate-to-strong current at 20-26m; inshore sites are milder
destinations.currents: Mild inshore; moderate to strong drift on the deeper offshore cleaning-station reefs
destinations.highlights: Year-round reef manta cleaning stations at Manta Reef and Giants Castle, whale sharks off the beach Oct-Mar, and migrating humpbacks Jun-Oct, all reached by RIB launches through the surf

Jan.marine_life: Whale sharks, resident mantas, reef sharks
Jan.conditions: Warm wet season, warmest water, 20-30m viz

Feb.conditions: Warm wet season, occasional rain, strong viz

Mar.marine_life: Whale sharks tapering, mantas, reef sharks
Mar.conditions: End of wet season, warm water, good viz

Apr.conditions: Drying out, calm seas, clear water

May.marine_life: Mantas on cleaning stations, reef sharks

Jun.conditions: Dry winter onset, windier, cooler water

Jul.conditions: Dry but windy, cooler water, viz can drop to 5-15m

Aug.marine_life: Humpback whale peak, mantas, reef sharks
Aug.conditions: Coolest water, windy dry season, variable viz

Sep.conditions: Winds easing, water warming, viz improving

Oct.marine_life: Whale sharks returning, late humpbacks, mantas
Oct.conditions: Calm seas, warming water, clearing viz

Nov.conditions: Warm, early wet season, strong viz

dive_sites.name: Manta Reef
dive_sites.blurb: World-famous amphitheatre reef with two large manta cleaning stations; a north-to-south drift best suited to divers with current experience

dive_sites.name: Giants Castle
dive_sites.blurb: Deep drift over walls, ridges and drop-offs with a second reef manta cleaning station; for advanced divers with deep-drift experience

dive_sites.name: The Office
dive_sites.blurb: Offshore reef about 10km out with clearer water; sandy bowl and ridge patrolled by whitetip, grey reef and leopard sharks plus honeycomb and marble rays

dive_sites.name: Salon
dive_sites.blurb: Relaxed shallow reef of stone boulders and sand famed for its cleaner-shrimp stations and easy conditions

dive_sites.name: Amazon
dive_sites.blurb: Coral-covered inshore reef with dense fish life, moray eels and frequent turtle and ray sightings

dive_sites.name: Sherwood Forest
dive_sites.blurb: Reef named for its forest of soft corals and sea fans, a reliable spot for reef sharks and passing mantas

destinations.image_credit: Photo: jon hanson (CC BY-SA 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Larnaca
destinations.current_note: Sheltered Larnaca Bay is usually calm; mild drift and surge can build on exposed reefs and inside the Zenobia when wind picks up, and deeper penetration carries silt-out and depth hazards.
destinations.currents: mild
destinations.highlights: The Zenobia, a 172m roll-on/roll-off ferry that sank in 1980 and now lies on her port side from 16m to a 42m seabed, is the centrepiece and ranks among the world's great wreck dives, its cargo of articulated lorries still chained to the deck. Beyond it Larnaca Bay strings together more accessible wrecks and reefs, while day trips reach the caves, tunnels and arches of Cape Greco. Warm, clear summer water and an easy shore-and-boat mix make it a year-round Mediterranean base that peaks from June to October.

Jan.marine_life: Quiet cool-water months; grouper, moray and bream hold on the wrecks while pelagic activity thins.
Jan.conditions: Coolest sea of the year around 16-17C calls for a full 5mm plus hood and gloves; winter fronts can cut boat access to the Zenobia, though calm windows still dive well.

Feb.marine_life: Resident wreck fish, moray eels and octopus stay active; few pelagics in the coldest water.
Feb.conditions: Coldest water of the year near 16C; 5mm-plus exposure protection needed and weather windows are short between fronts.

Mar.marine_life: Life stirs as water warms; nudibranchs are abundant and grouper and bream become more visible.
Mar.conditions: Season reopens with settling weather; still cool at roughly 17C so a 5mm suit and hood stay sensible.

Apr.marine_life: Warming reefs bring more fish onto the wrecks; barracuda and amberjack start to appear over the Alexandria.
Apr.conditions: Milder, more settled conditions and generally clear water; a 5mm suit suits most divers.

May.marine_life: Marine life picks up strongly; schooling barracuda and amberjack, grouper and turtles become common sightings.
May.conditions: Pleasant water near 21C and calm seas open reliable boat access to the Zenobia; 5mm suit.

Jun.marine_life: Peak activity begins: barracuda and amberjack school over the wrecks, grouper hold on structure and turtles pass through.
Jun.conditions: Warm settled summer with excellent visibility and easy access to every site; 3mm suffices in the warming water.

Jul.marine_life: Full summer abundance around the Zenobia and reefs; barracuda, amberjack, grouper, moray and turtles.
Jul.conditions: Warmest, calmest stretch of the year at around 27C with long clear days; a 3mm suit is plenty.

Aug.marine_life: High-summer marine life at its richest across wrecks and reefs; pelagics, grouper, morays and turtles.
Aug.conditions: Peak warmth near 27-28C, glassy seas and top visibility; 3mm suit and busy dive traffic on the Zenobia.

Sep.marine_life: Still-warm water keeps life abundant; large barracuda and amberjack schools, grouper and frequent turtle sightings.
Sep.conditions: Warm, stable and clear with fewer crowds than midsummer; 3mm suit remains comfortable.

Oct.marine_life: Marine life stays lively as the water slowly cools; wrecks still teem with fish and pelagics linger.
Oct.conditions: Warm water near 24C and generally settled seas; slip back into a 5mm as autumn advances.

Nov.marine_life: Cooling reefs quiet down but resident grouper, moray and bream remain reliable on the wrecks.
Nov.conditions: Water around 22C and the first autumn fronts arrive; a 5mm suit and watch on the weather for boat trips.

Dec.marine_life: Winter lull with mostly resident wreck fish, moray eels and octopus; little pelagic movement.
Dec.conditions: Cooling toward 19C with unsettled spells; 5mm plus hood and calm-window planning for the Zenobia.

dive_sites.name: The Zenobia
dive_sites.blurb: A 172m roll-on/roll-off ferry that sank in 1980 and now lies on her port side, ranked among the world's finest wreck dives. Recreational routes explore the upper hull and twin propellers while deeper penetration into the car decks and cargo of chained lorries is a technical, advanced-only undertaking.

dive_sites.name: The Alexandria
dive_sites.blurb: A fishing trawler that went down in a 2006 storm while under tow, now sitting upright and richly encrusted. Schools of barracuda and amberjack swirl overhead while grouper, triggerfish and nudibranchs work the hull.

dive_sites.name: HMS Cricket
dive_sites.blurb: A WWI-era British river gunboat used as an RAF target and sunk in 1947, resting upside down with a sandy swim-through beneath the hull. Grouper, moray eels, bream and lionfish shelter around the wreckage.

dive_sites.name: Green Bay
dive_sites.blurb: A shallow, sheltered bay with seagrass and submerged statues that is ideal for shore entries, training and relaxed second dives. Cuttlefish, wrasse, bream and the occasional turtle keep it lively.

dive_sites.name: Cape Greco Caves
dive_sites.blurb: A day-trip playground of caves, tunnels, arches and rocky reef along a dramatic headland. Sunlit swim-throughs shelter octopus, moray eels, grouper and passing barracuda.

dive_sites.name: Cape Greco - The Caverns
dive_sites.blurb: Shallower cavern and gully diving on the Cape Greco coast with easy topography and good light. Bream, wrasse, octopus and nudibranchs populate the rock, making it a strong all-round reef dive.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Jetlife2 (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Kaş
destinations.current_note: Most sheltered reefs are calm, but exposed sites like the Canyon can run a brisk southerly current across the shallow gorge before the wall.
destinations.currents: Generally light around the harbour reefs; moderate at exposed headlands and the Canyon, where a southerly flow may need to be crossed to reach the walls.
destinations.highlights: The premier dive base on Turkey's Turquoise Coast, with 30-plus sites minutes from the harbour: dramatic walls and the Canyon, the intact sunken DC-3 Dakota, a full-size replica of the Bronze Age Uluburun wreck, amphora fields and swim-through caverns, all in famously clear warm-summer water.

Jan.marine_life: Reef fish and grouper present but subdued; fewer pelagics in the cold water.
Jan.conditions: Cool 16-17C sea, thick winter suits or drysuits; some operators run reduced or by-request schedules.

Feb.marine_life: Quietest month for marine life; resident morays, octopus and grouper in crevices.
Feb.conditions: Coldest water of the year near 16C; winter winds can cancel boats, limited operator availability.

Mar.marine_life: Life beginning to stir as water slowly warms; grouper and wrasse on the reefs.
Mar.conditions: Still cold at 17C with 7mm suits or drysuits; unsettled early-spring weather, quiet town.

Apr.marine_life: Reefs waking up; grouper, moray and octopus active, occasional amberjack.
Apr.conditions: Season opening; sea near 18C wants a thick 7mm suit, mixed but improving weather.

May.marine_life: Marine life building well; amberjack, barracuda and grouper around the walls.
May.conditions: Comfortable 21C sea, 5mm suits, generally settled conditions and full operator schedules.

Jun.marine_life: Abundant fish life; grouper, amberjack, barracuda and first summer turtles.
Jun.conditions: Warm 25C water, 3-5mm suits, calm sunny days and excellent visibility.

Jul.marine_life: Peak activity; loggerhead turtles, amberjack schools, grouper and octopus common.
Jul.conditions: Warm 27C sea ideal for 3mm suits, calm seas, superb clarity; busiest season.

Aug.marine_life: Rich marine life with frequent turtle sightings and pelagics on the reefs.
Aug.conditions: Warmest water near 28C, thin 3mm suits, flat calm days and long clear visibility.

Sep.marine_life: Excellent life continues; turtles, amberjack, barracuda and abundant reef fish.
Sep.conditions: Still warm 26C sea, 3-5mm suits, settled weather and outstanding visibility; a top month.

Oct.marine_life: Strong marine life lingering into autumn; grouper, moray, octopus and passing pelagics.
Oct.conditions: Pleasant 23C water, 5mm suits, mostly calm with the season winding down late month.

Nov.marine_life: Life thinning as water cools; resident grouper, moray and octopus remain.
Nov.conditions: Cooling 20C sea, 5-7mm suits, more unsettled weather and shorter operator schedules.

Dec.marine_life: Quiet cool-water reefs; grouper, moray and octopus tucked into structure.
Dec.conditions: Cold 18C sea heading into winter, thick suits; limited winter boat availability.

dive_sites.name: The Canyon
dive_sites.blurb: Kaş signature dive: cross a shallow gorge, often against a southerly current, into a rift between two steep walls dropping past 20m, cloaked in yellow cluster anemones and sponges with a small cave and red shrimp.

dive_sites.name: Big Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A classic sloping reef and wall system with terraces, cracks and overhangs; grouper, moray, octopus and passing amberjack against superb Turquoise-Coast visibility.

dive_sites.name: DC-3 Dakota Plane Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A WWII-era Dakota transport plane deliberately sunk as an artificial reef; the intact fuselage sits on sand at around 25m, now colonised by fish, morays and encrusting growth, with a swim-through interior for trained wreck divers.

dive_sites.name: Uluburun Replica (Kaş Archaeopark)
dive_sites.blurb: A full-size replica of the Late Bronze Age Uluburun ship laid out in Hidayet Bay with copper ingots and amphorae, an accessible museum-style dive celebrating the region's underwater archaeology.

dive_sites.name: Flying Fish (Dimitri) Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A small coastal wreck on a rocky slope that has become a fish magnet, with amberjack, barracuda and grouper patrolling the structure and morays in the recesses.

dive_sites.name: Amphora Field
dive_sites.blurb: A scatter of ancient clay amphorae across a gentle reef, evidence of the coast's trading past, with octopus and moray sheltering inside the vessels.

dive_sites.name: Fener (Lighthouse) Cave
dive_sites.blurb: A dramatic wall pierced by caverns and swim-throughs near the lighthouse point, with shafts of light, lobster and shrimp in the dark recesses and grouper along the drop-off.

destinations.name: Ustica
destinations.current_note: Sheltered bays and pinnacles are usually calm, but exposed offshore seamounts like Secca della Colombara and the pinnacle at Scoglio del Medico can pick up moderate current when wind and swell build.
destinations.currents: variable
destinations.highlights: Italy's first Marine Protected Area (established 1986), where decades of protection have produced crystal water, walls draped in gorgonians and sponges, and unusually bold dusky groupers. Volcanic topography carves caves, arches and tunnels, headlined by the offshore basalt pinnacle Scoglio del Medico. Two underwater archaeological trails let divers hover over ancient anchors and amphorae in situ.

Jan.marine_life: Groupers and resident reef fish remain, but activity is subdued in the cold season and most operators are wound down for winter.
Jan.conditions: Cool, quiet winter water with occasional storms; only a handful of dives run for hardy locals.

Feb.marine_life: Sparse cold-water activity; resident groupers and morays hold in the caves.
Feb.conditions: Coldest sea of the year and winter swells; centres largely closed.

Mar.marine_life: Marine life slowly stirs as temperatures bottom out and begin to turn.
Mar.conditions: Still cool and weather-dependent; early operators start limited pre-season diving.

Apr.marine_life: Groupers active on the walls and nudibranchs plentiful in the cool, plankton-rich water.
Apr.conditions: Season warming up with generally settled spells; dive centres reopening.

May.marine_life: Groupers, barracuda and amberjack become reliable as the water warms; strong nudibranch showing.
May.conditions: Comfortable early-season diving with visibility climbing and calm days common.

Jun.marine_life: Bold dusky groupers, barracuda schools, amberjack and octopus across the sites.
Jun.conditions: Warm, clear and calm; excellent visibility and full operator schedules.

Jul.marine_life: Peak marine life: unafraid groupers, barracuda, amberjack, morays and octopus.
Jul.conditions: Warmest, clearest conditions with long settled spells and the busiest season.

Aug.marine_life: Big groupers and pelagic amberjack and barracuda at their most active.
Aug.conditions: Warmest water of the year and superb visibility; busiest month.

Sep.marine_life: Excellent action continues with groupers, barracuda and amberjack still abundant.
Sep.conditions: Still warm and clear with thinning crowds; often the connoisseur's month.

Oct.marine_life: Groupers and morays remain reliable while pelagic activity gradually eases.
Oct.conditions: Water still warm at around 22C; visibility strong on calm days as the season winds down.

Nov.marine_life: Resident groupers and reef life persist as the water cools.
Nov.conditions: End-of-season diving; increasingly weather-dependent as most operators close.

Dec.marine_life: Quiet cold-season reef with groupers and morays holding in the caves.
Dec.conditions: Cool winter water and storms; centres largely closed with only occasional local dives.

dive_sites.name: Scoglio del Medico
dive_sites.blurb: Signature offshore basalt outcrop off the west coast, riddled with grottoes, gorges, tunnels and canyons. Enclosed passages around 15-18m lead into a cave through the rock and the large cavity known as La Balena, with careful penetration to about 26m.

dive_sites.name: Grotta dei Gamberi
dive_sites.blurb: Cave near Punta Gavazzi at the southern tip whose walls glow orange with countless narval shrimp. An advanced dive averaging 20-25m, with its main entrance deeper at around 40m.

dive_sites.name: Secca della Colombara
dive_sites.blurb: The finest dive on the north coast, west of Grotta dell'Oro, famed for vast gorgonian fans and Ustica's most beautiful sponges on an exposed offshore seamount that can catch current.

dive_sites.name: Punta Galera
dive_sites.blurb: One of the most popular and versatile sites, an easy sheltered bay with a green cave, turquoise water and frequent sightings of barracuda, amberjack and grouper.

dive_sites.name: Punta Cavazzi Archaeological Trail
dive_sites.blurb: The first underwater archaeological itinerary set in the Mediterranean, where ancient anchors of various eras rest roughly where they were found, offering an easy plunge back in time.

dive_sites.name: Punta Falconiera Archaeological Trail
dive_sites.blurb: The deeper of Ustica's two heritage itineraries, descending toward 30m to hover over ancient anchors and amphorae in situ within the protected zone.

dive_sites.name: Grotta dell'Oro
dive_sites.blurb: A colourful volcanic cave-and-wall dive on the north coast, its rock walls carpeted in sponges and encrusting life typical of the well-protected reserve.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Berlinschneid (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Vis Island
destinations.current_note: Sheltered reefs and inshore wrecks are usually slack; deeper offshore wrecks and exposed points can run when wind and tide align, so plan drops around slack water.
destinations.currents: Generally light with mostly negligible flow inshore, picking up to moderate on exposed sites and in deeper open water.
destinations.highlights: The B-17 Flying Fortress, one of the best-preserved WWII aircraft wrecks in the world, resting intact on sand at 65-72m for technical divers. Historic steamer wrecks including the shallow Teti and the ~105m Greek cargo ship Vassilios T., still laden with coal. The Blue Cave of Biševo, where midday sun floods the chamber with electric blue light. Reefs, drop-off walls and swim-through caves rich in Adriatic life around Vis and its outer islets.

Jan.marine_life: Sparse and slow in cold water; grouper and conger hold in cover, life is quiet.
Jan.conditions: Cold sea near 13-14C and winter storms; few operators run.

Feb.marine_life: Coldest, quietest month with minimal fish activity on the reefs.
Feb.conditions: Coldest sea of the year around 13C with unsettled weather.

Mar.marine_life: Still cold; scattered grouper, scorpionfish and octopus in the shallows.
Mar.conditions: Cool sea near 14C, variable spring weather, limited boat cover.

Apr.marine_life: Reefs waking up as water warms; octopus and moray more active.
Apr.conditions: Cool water around 15C, calmer spells, operators reopening.

May.marine_life: Active reefs with grouper, lobster and conger out; spring plankton can bloom.
May.conditions: Warming sea near 18C and settling weather; season underway.

Jun.marine_life: Full Adriatic cast on the reefs and wrecks; grouper and lobster common.
Jun.conditions: Warm sea around 21C, calm settled conditions, clear water.

Jul.marine_life: Prolific reef and wreck life; conger and grouper reliable, octopus in the cracks.
Jul.conditions: Warm sea near 24C, calm summer weather, excellent clarity.

Aug.marine_life: Peak abundance across every site; wrecks and walls busy with fish.
Aug.conditions: Warmest sea around 25C and flat calm; busiest, clearest month.

Sep.marine_life: Warm water keeps life active; grouper, lobster and octopus plentiful.
Sep.conditions: Still-warm sea near 23C, calm settled weather, superb visibility.

Oct.marine_life: Reefs stay lively before the cool-down; grouper and conger active.
Oct.conditions: Mild sea around 20C, occasional autumn fronts, still very clear.

Nov.marine_life: Cooling reefs slowing down; fish retreating into cover.
Nov.conditions: Cooler sea near 18C and more unsettled weather; fewer boats.

Dec.marine_life: Cold and quiet; sparse activity on the reefs.
Dec.conditions: Cold sea around 15C and winter storms; minimal operations.

dive_sites.name: B-17 Flying Fortress
dive_sites.blurb: A near-intact WWII American bomber that ditched off Vis in 1944, sitting upright on sand as one of the best-preserved aircraft wrecks anywhere; a deep technical dive only.

dive_sites.name: Vassilios T.
dive_sites.blurb: A ~105m Greek cargo steamer resting on its port side, still holding its cargo of coal; among the largest wrecks in the Adriatic and needing several dives to see.

dive_sites.name: Teti
dive_sites.blurb: A broken-up steamship on a slope where conger eels lurk in the plating by day; the shallower sections make an accessible, life-rich wreck dive.

dive_sites.name: Blue Cave (Bisevo)
dive_sites.blurb: A sea cave off nearby Bisevo island where midday sunlight refracts through an underwater opening to bathe the chamber in glowing blue light.

dive_sites.name: Stoncica Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A sloping reef and drop-off near the Stoncica lighthouse with grouper, scorpionfish and octopus among the rocks and gorgonians.

dive_sites.name: B-24 Liberator
dive_sites.blurb: The scattered remains of an American four-engine WWII bomber on sand, a deep recreational wreck for experienced divers exploring the debris field.

dive_sites.name: Ravnik / Green Cave
dive_sites.blurb: A through-cave on the islet of Ravnik lit green from a ceiling opening, with an easy swim-through and reef life around the entrance.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Borut Furlan (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Chania (Crete)
destinations.current_note: Generally sheltered water on the north coast; mild currents possible around exposed capes, islets and the cave entrances, stronger with meltemi wind.
destinations.currents: mild, occasionally moderate at exposed sites
destinations.highlights: Western Crete's main dive base, best known for the submerged Elephant Cave (Elefas) at Cape Drepano with its fossil stalactite-and-stalagmite wall and Ice Age elephant-bone finds. Clear blue Aegean/Mediterranean water, dramatic caverns, walls and reefs, plus a few wreck dives, wrapped around a long April-to-November summer season and quiet, cool winters.

Jan.marine_life: Quiet cold-season reefs; resident grouper, moray and octopus still about
Jan.conditions: Coolest water of the year and winter swell; few boats running

Feb.marine_life: Sparse winter life on the walls and caverns
Feb.conditions: Cold sea and variable wind; mostly shore dives when weather allows

Mar.marine_life: Life slowly returning as water begins to warm

Apr.marine_life: Reefs waking up; grouper, moray and octopus more active
Apr.conditions: Season opening; cool but clearing water, calmer spells

May.marine_life: Active reefs and walls, barracuda schools forming
May.conditions: Warming, settled water with strong visibility

Jun.marine_life: Abundant Mediterranean life on caverns and reefs
Jun.conditions: Warm, calm early-summer diving, excellent clarity

Jul.marine_life: Peak abundance; grouper, moray, barracuda and octopus common
Jul.conditions: Warm, clear water; occasional afternoon meltemi wind

Aug.marine_life: Prolific reef, wall and cavern life
Aug.conditions: Warmest sea of the year, superb visibility; meltemi can build

Sep.marine_life: Rich life and best big-fish activity of the year
Sep.conditions: Warm, settled water and outstanding clarity

Oct.marine_life: Still lively as the sea slowly cools
Oct.conditions: Mild water, calm autumn spells, great visibility

Nov.conditions: Cooling water, more wind and swell; season winding down

Dec.conditions: Cold water and winter weather; limited boat diving

dive_sites.name: Elephant Cave (Elefas)
dive_sites.blurb: Chania's signature dive at Cape Drepano off Souda Bay. The entrance sits around 10 m and opens into a large chamber with a spectacular fused stalactite-and-stalagmite wall; fossilised bones of an extinct dwarf elephant were found here. Broad and shallow with air pockets, but still an overhead environment for trained divers.

dive_sites.name: Menies (Diktynna)
dive_sites.blurb: Reef and boulder dive off the remote Menies coast on the Rodopou peninsula, near the ancient Diktynna sanctuary. Clear water, sloping rock terraces and good numbers of grouper, moray and octopus.

dive_sites.name: Islet of Marathi
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered, scenic reef dive off the Akrotiri peninsula with easy access and crystal-clear water, ideal for training and relaxed exploration among rocky reef and seagrass.

dive_sites.name: Ammoudi
dive_sites.blurb: Easy shore and shallow reef dive in some of the clearest turquoise water in the area; gentle rocky reef with damselfish, octopus and nudibranchs, popular for check-out and refresher dives.

dive_sites.name: Lighthouse Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A dramatic drop-off wall dive, one of the finer walls in the Chania area, with cracks and overhangs sheltering grouper and moray and passing barracuda in the blue.

dive_sites.name: Seal Cave
dive_sites.blurb: A rocky cavern dive named for the monk seals occasionally seen along this coast; swim-throughs and shafts of light with cavern-dwelling life on the walls.

dive_sites.name: Dia Island
dive_sites.blurb: Boat dive around the uninhabited, Natura 2000-protected islet off Crete's north coast, with exceptionally clear water, healthy reef and wall terrain and depths to suit both newer and experienced divers.

destinations.name: Bat Islands
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm full (thermoclines can plunge below 21°C even when the surface is warm)
destinations.current_note: Exposed offshore pinnacles with strong, variable currents, surge and downcurrents around the rock; the ~45km open-water crossing from Playas del Coco can be rough. Advanced certification and solid buoyancy are expected.
destinations.highlights: Costa Rica's premier bull shark dive: at the Big Scare (El Gran Susto) a dozen or more 2.5-3.5m bull sharks cruise volcanic pinnacles at 20-30m, joined by giant Pacific mantas, squadrons of eagle and cow-nose rays and dense schools of jacks and snapper. A committing offshore day-boat trip from the Guanacaste coast, best in the warm green season.

Jan.marine_life: Dry-season winds and upwelling bring cold thermoclines but clearer water; rays, turtles and big schools of fish, only occasional bull sharks.
Jan.conditions: Windy Papagayo season; often cooler water and stronger surface chop, but better average visibility.

Feb.marine_life: Peak upwelling clarity with eagle and cow-nose rays, jacks and turtles; bull shark sightings sparse this month.
Feb.conditions: Strong offshore winds can cancel the crossing; coldest thermoclines of the year.

Mar.marine_life: Rays, turtles and reef fish remain; bull sharks largely absent before the green season builds.
Mar.conditions: Windy and cool with variable currents; trips weather-dependent.

Apr.marine_life: Transition month as water warms; mantas and rays about, early bull shark encounters possible late in the month.
Apr.conditions: Winds easing, water warming; visibility still generally good.

May.marine_life: Green season begins and bull sharks return to the Big Scare, with mantas, eagle rays and heavy schooling fish.
May.conditions: Warm water and calmer surface, though rain can cut visibility on some days.

Jun.marine_life: Reliable bull shark aggregations plus giant Pacific mantas, rays and baitballs of jacks and snapper.
Jun.conditions: Warm green-season water with strong currents at the pinnacles; visibility swings dive to dive.

Jul.marine_life: Prime bull shark month with a dozen or more animals common at the Big Scare, alongside mantas and rays.
Jul.conditions: Warmest water of the year; brief afternoon downpours, variable currents and visibility.

Aug.marine_life: Peak bull shark density continues with mantas, cow-nose ray squadrons and turtles.
Aug.conditions: Warm and active green-season diving; visibility can drop with runoff but the action stays high.

Sep.marine_life: Bull sharks still abundant with excellent big-animal odds through the wet peak.
Sep.conditions: Wettest stretch; occasional rough crossings and reduced visibility, but shark encounters are consistent.

Oct.marine_life: Strong late-season bull shark presence tapering off, with mantas and rays still frequent.
Oct.conditions: Green season winding down; warm water, variable visibility and lingering rain.

Nov.marine_life: Bull shark odds ease as the season closes; rays, turtles and schooling fish remain reliable.
Nov.conditions: Transition to drier weather; water still warm, visibility improving.

Dec.marine_life: Rays, turtles and big schools with clearer water returning; bull sharks now scarce.
Dec.conditions: Papagayo winds returning bring cooler thermoclines and choppier crossings but sharper visibility.

dive_sites.name: Big Scare (El Gran Susto)
dive_sites.blurb: The signature site: exposed rocks breaking the surface where six to a dozen large bull sharks patrol the volcanic pinnacles, joined by giant mantas, jacks, snapper and the occasional sailfish or marlin.

dive_sites.name: Black Rock (Bajo Negro)
dive_sites.blurb: A steep pinnacle rising from around 30m toward the surface off Santa Rosa, drawing squadrons of eagle rays, dense schooling fish, occasional bull sharks and cruising manta rays.

dive_sites.name: Punta Gorda
dive_sites.blurb: A rocky point known for gliding groups of common and spotted eagle rays over the boulders, with turtles and reef fish in shallower water.

dive_sites.name: The Point
dive_sites.blurb: Current-swept ridges and boulder gardens holding schooling grunts, snapper and pompano, with rays and turtles working the edges.

dive_sites.name: Coral Gardens
dive_sites.blurb: A slightly more sheltered rocky-reef area used when conditions are rougher, with morays, invertebrates and reef fish and lighter current than the sharky pinnacles.

dive_sites.name: Bat Island Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A sloping wall along the islands where big pelagic schools, rays and passing bull sharks show up in the current, best dived on slack.

destinations.name: Coiba
destinations.access: Day-boat or multi-day trips from Santa Catalina (about 1 to 1.5 hours by boat to the nearest sites); remote northern Contreras Islands reached by liveaboard-style multi-night trips lodging at Pixvae or park islands.
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm full wetsuit (thermoclines can drop to 16-20C; rental centres often stock only 3mm, so bring your own)
destinations.current_note: Exposed seamounts and pinnacles run strong, sometimes shifting currents that draw pelagics; negative or semi-negative entries and blue-water ascents are common.
destinations.highlights: A Pacific UNESCO marine park where cool, nutrient-rich upwelling feeds schooling scalloped hammerheads (best March-July), resident whitetip reef sharks, seasonal whale sharks and mantas, plus walls of jacks and barracuda over coral-clad seamounts.

Jan.marine_life: Dry-season upwelling fuels big schools of jacks and barracuda, whitetip reef sharks and the start of whale shark chances
Jan.conditions: Calm seas, clear water, cool thermoclines below 15-20m

Feb.marine_life: Whale shark window continues, dense fish schools, turtles and mobulas over the seamounts
Feb.conditions: Dry, calm and clear; strong currents on exposed pinnacles

Mar.marine_life: Scalloped hammerhead schools build at pinnacles and Contreras; whale sharks still possible
Mar.conditions: Pronounced thermoclines begin bringing colder, plankton-rich water

Apr.marine_life: Prime hammerhead season with the biggest schools, plus whitetips, mantas and mobula rays
Apr.conditions: End of dry season, cool nutrient-rich currents, variable viz on thermoclines

May.marine_life: Hammerheads still schooling; abundant pelagic action as upwelling peaks
May.conditions: First rains arrive late month; strong currents and cool water

Jun.marine_life: Hammerheads best sought at the northern Contreras Islands; big-fish action holds
Jun.conditions: Rainy season underway, seas and visibility turn variable

Jul.marine_life: Hammerhead numbers taper; whitetip reef sharks, turtles and rays remain reliable
Jul.conditions: Wet and variable; run-off can cloud the water, third dives weather-dependent

Aug.marine_life: Resident reef life, whitetips and mobulas; passing mantas at plankton-rich sites
Aug.conditions: Peak rains, greener plankton-laden water, calmer seas between storms

Sep.marine_life: Reef sharks, turtles and macro life; fewer big schools as upwelling eases
Sep.conditions: Wettest stretch, reduced and inconsistent visibility

Oct.marine_life: Steady reef life and rays; pelagic action quietest of the year
Oct.conditions: Still rainy with murkier, plankton-rich water

Nov.marine_life: Fish schools and whitetips rebuild as conditions clear toward dry season
Nov.conditions: Rains ease late month; visibility improving in transition

Dec.marine_life: Dry-season return brings big schools, whitetips and the first whale shark sightings
Dec.conditions: Calming seas and clearing water; cool thermoclines at depth

dive_sites.name: Wahoo Rock
dive_sites.blurb: Coiba at its wildest: an offshore pinnacle of coral-, sponge- and fan-covered ridges swept by strong, shifting currents. Negative entries drop you into schooling jacks, barracuda, whitetips and the season's hammerheads.

dive_sites.name: Don Juan
dive_sites.blurb: A gentler rock-and-reef site of boulders and ledges hosting whitetip reef sharks resting on the sand, turtles, morays and dense reef fish, with occasional mobulas cruising the blue.

dive_sites.name: Frijoles
dive_sites.blurb: A colourful sloping reef good for wide-angle and macro alike: seahorses, frogfish, octopus and morays among the coral, with turtles and schooling snapper overhead.

dive_sites.name: Bajo Uno
dive_sites.blurb: A current-swept underwater pinnacle that concentrates pelagic life, from schooling hammerheads and big-fish tornadoes to passing mantas; entries and ascents are made in open blue water.

dive_sites.name: Buffet (La Viuda)
dive_sites.blurb: A signature Coiba pinnacle whose tip breaks the surface, wrapped in massive fish schools; plankton draws mantas and mobulas, while whitetips and turtles patrol the rocks.

dive_sites.name: Iglesia
dive_sites.blurb: Cathedral-like rock arches and swim-throughs give the site its name, sheltering reef fish, lobster and morays with pelagics passing along the outer walls.

dive_sites.name: Contreras Islands (Montana Rusa)
dive_sites.blurb: The park's far-north hotspot, reached only on multi-day trips: strong currents deliver the best odds at schooling hammerheads alongside whitetips, mobula rays and green turtles.
